---
title: "Can primary care funding grow before hospitals have to?"
subtitle: "GTPCNZ public aggregate validation briefing"
author: "Dylan Mordaunt"
date: "2026-06-10"
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
    preview-links: auto
    theme: simple
---

## The question

Can New Zealand design primary care funding so lower-cost upstream care can grow before need becomes hospital demand?

**Boundary:** public aggregate validation only. Not a patient-level forecast. Not linked-data calibrated.

## What changed in v1.8.1

- Public-only runtime source path.
- Registered public source snapshots and hashes.
- Parameter traceability.
- Public aggregate calibration.
- CAL-G-001 through CAL-G-007 gate reporting.
- Structural uncertainty and VOI surfaces.
- Claim-gated Streamlit cockpit and release model card.

## Current validation status

| Field | Status |
|---|---|
| Claim level | `empirically_supported_if_gated` |
| Calibration status | `public_aggregate_validated` |
| Scope | registered public aggregate gates |
| Still excluded | fiscal, ED, hospital-demand, workforce, implementation, causal claims |

## The policy architecture result

The model does **not** say "fee-for-service wins".

It says:

> Capitation reweighting improves allocation, but does not necessarily remove the marginal supply constraint.

## Why one lever is not enough

| Lever | Helps | Risk if isolated |
|---|---|---|
| Capitation | enrolment, continuity, population responsibility | weak marginal activity signal |
| Scheduled activity payment | eligible contact growth | gaming, fragmentation |
| Place accountability | equity and hard-to-reach populations | local governance complexity |
| Co-payment protection | access and legitimacy | fiscal exposure if unmanaged |
| Audit/data | control and learning | compliance burden |

## Strongest design pattern

Retain and reweight capitation.

Add controlled scheduled eligible medical activity payments.

Tie activity growth to:

- place accountability;
- urgent and ambulance alternatives;
- scope-enabled providers;
- co-payment protection;
- audit and data visibility.

## Public validation gates

| Gate | Family | Status |
|---|---|---|
| CAL-G-001 | baseline aggregate reproduction | passed |
| CAL-G-002 | temporal holdout | passed |
| CAL-G-003 | geographic holdout | passed |
| CAL-G-004 | subgroup gradient holdout | passed |
| CAL-G-005 | public policy-shock plausibility | passed |
| CAL-G-006 | posterior predictive checks | passed |
| CAL-G-007 | claim downgrade | passed |

## What the model cannot claim

Do not use it to claim:

- precise fiscal savings;
- ED reductions;
- hospital-demand reductions;
- workforce effects;
- implementation impacts;
- causal effects.

## Evidence priorities

The next evidence value is in:

- district and subgroup persistence checks;
- access and urgent/ambulance public time series;
- activity-sensitive funding without gaming;
- place-accountability effects;
- PHO function versus transaction-cost evidence.

## Practical policy implication

Treat primary care funding as an architecture.

Do not ask capitation to do every job.

Do not add uncapped activity payment without controls.

Do not treat urgent care and ambulance as separate from primary care access.

## Recommendation

Use the model as a transparent decision-support and evidence-prioritisation tool.

Run policy design as a staged public-learning programme:

1. Keep capitation and reweight it.
2. Test controlled eligible activity payment.
3. Require place accountability.
4. Protect patients from co-payment harm.
5. Publish enough aggregate data to learn.

## Closing line

The result is not a forecast.

It is a disciplined warning:

> A primary care system that cannot grow upstream will keep buying rescue downstream.
